description: A 'henora' is the conditioned limit of what a system can hold, process, or deliver. A cistern may have ample capacity while nearly empty; a cable may reach its capacity without holding anything in the ordinary sense. This technical limit belongs to the system and its conditions. A person's ability or standing is another question.
articulatory_notes: Open the glottis for 'h' and move into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and round into stressed true-mid back 'o'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and finish on central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The breathy opening moves into the rounded chamber of stressed 'no', then 'ra' rolls outward. The mouth finds room and then its reach, an apt physical memory for a limit under stated conditions.
usage_notes: The noun may be mass or countable and is often possessed by a system. Exact values and units remain in a separate source record. 'phelu' (hold) reports an act or relation, 'pheno' (full) reports current contents relative to a boundary, and 'puro' (strength) concerns force or resolve.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Capacity lets a community plan within actual material limits instead of treating infrastructure as an endless promise.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: People have long understood tools, stores, waterways, and shared works through what they can hold or sustain under real conditions.

semantic_domains.cognition: a specified limit whose interpretation depends on object, conditions, and time
semantic_domains.creation: a design property used in sizing and comparing infrastructure
semantic_domains.physical: a conditioned amount or rate a material system can hold or handle
